title: Cancer cachexia / weight-loss awareness (general, non-prescriptive)
context: Cancer cachexia and malnutrition are associated with worse treatment tolerance, more dose reductions, and worse survival — making them among the highest-stakes nutrition topics in this project. However, the management of cachexia involves individualized clinical decisions that must not be presented as general advice. This backlog topic covers general, non-prescriptive awareness: why eating enough matters, early warning signs to report to the care team, and a strong defer-to-dietitian framing. Requires oncologist review in addition to RDN/CSO review due to clinical loading. Content is education only — no personalized targets or dietary prescriptions.
objective: Author cancer cachexia and treatment-related weight-loss awareness content at a general, non-prescriptive level: what cachexia is, why eating enough during treatment matters, warning signs to report to the care team, and a strong defer-to-oncology-dietitian message. Does not prescribe calorie targets, protein goals, oral supplements, or individualized nutrition support plans.
acceptanceCriteria: Content explains cancer cachexia and treatment-related weight loss at a plain-language, general-awareness level; explains why maintaining adequate intake during treatment matters and what the consequences of malnutrition can include — cited to authoritative sources (NCI/PDQ, ESPEN, ASPEN, etc.)

Content includes clear warning signs to report promptly to the care team (significant unintentional weight loss, inability to eat, rapid deterioration) with a red-flag escalation block routing to the care team

Content is strictly non-prescriptive: does not prescribe calorie targets, protein or macronutrient goals, oral nutritional supplement brands or doses, or individualized nutrition support plans; all individualized decisions deferred to the oncology dietitian and team
Not-medical-advice banner present; every claim cited to a license-verified source; citation-coverage CI gate passes; content passes grade 8 or below readability check; staleness check passes

Credentialed oncologist sign-off recorded (required due to clinical loading of cachexia content); credentialed oncology RDN/CSO sign-off recorded; patient-advocate sign-off for tone and readability recorded
output: A plain-language, source-cited, oncologist- and RDN/CSO-signed-off cancer cachexia awareness document, non-prescriptive and with a strong defer-to-dietitian message, merged into the content corpus and passing all CI gates.
